_meta.notes: alt1_vibe arrays are ordered primary-first (max 2). alt2_moment is used as both the timeline axis (Alt 2) and the 'when' filter (Alt 4). alt4_feeling is the second filter axis for Alt 4.

apps.title: Live "How Well Do You Know Us?" Trivia
apps.description: A live multiplayer quiz where every question is about the couple's real relationship, played on guests' phones with a leaderboard projected on the reception screen.
apps.why: Guests learn surprising things about the couple they didn't know, and the winner earns the right to give an actual toast — the game has stakes built in.
cool_features: 15 questions, 20 seconds each — the whole game takes 8 minutes and never drags
Live leaderboard on the big screen with guests' real names, not usernames
Table-level scoring on top of individual scoring — head table vs. college friends becomes a subplot
Couple pre-records 5-second video reactions for each reveal ("nope, that was actually a Bumble opener from October 2020")
Winner gets an unscheduled 60-second toast at the mic
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: Trivia forces the whole room to focus on the same thing at the same time. Nobody's scrolling — they're screaming at their tablemates. It's 8 minutes bounded, not 80.

non_app_version.description: Printed trivia sheets at each table, host reads questions from a mic, tables score themselves on paper.
non_app_version.needed: No — pub-quiz style works.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — live leaderboards, video reactions, and per-guest scoring turn it from a party game into a spectacle.

apps.title: Venue Scavenger Hunt with Their Love Story
apps.description: QR codes hidden around the venue each unlock a chapter of the couple's love story — cocktail hour becomes a treasure hunt through their actual lives.
apps.why: Every stop has a personal reason for being there, shy guests have something to do, and completing all chapters reveals a secret message from the couple.
cool_features: Each QR code lives at a spot with real meaning (bar = where they had their first drink together, garden = where he proposed)
Chapters unlock in order — you can't skip ahead, so guests physically move through the venue
Photo challenges at some stops: "take a selfie here and it appears in the couple's album"
Completing all chapters unlocks a private thank-you video from the couple
Leaderboard of "first to finish" earns a small prize at the bar
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: The hunt sends guests walking, talking, and exploring instead of clustering by the bar. Phone use is brief at each stop — the reward is the chapter, then heads back up.

non_app_version.description: Printed clue cards handed out at arrival, physical envelopes taped to venue landmarks with the next clue inside.
non_app_version.needed: No — paper hunts are a wedding classic.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — QR chapters can carry video, voice notes, and photos in the couple's own words that a printed card physically cannot.

apps.title: Anniversary Time Capsule
apps.description: Guests record short video messages sealed until the 1st, 5th, or 10th anniversary — the app locks them and delivers them automatically on the right date.
apps.why: A grandparent can leave a message for the couple to open when they might no longer be around; that possibility transforms how every guest records.
cool_features: Guests pick the delivery year — 1, 5, 10, 25, even 50 — and the app schedules the email for the actual anniversary date
Guests can attach a photo of themselves from tonight, so future-them sees present-them
Special "for the kids" tier — messages delivered when the couple's future children turn 18
Discreet re-record button — no bad takes get sent
The couple can also write messages to themselves on the same schedule
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: It's a quiet, thoughtful submission — guests do it when they have a natural moment. No screen presence, no interruptions. It's a gift the wedding gives the couple for decades.

non_app_version.description: Envelopes labelled "Open in 1 year / 5 / 10 / 25" at each table; couple stores them in a keepsake box.
non_app_version.needed: No — dated envelopes are a beautiful, tactile tradition.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes for the 25- and 50-year tiers where paper reliably gets lost. Scheduled delivery is the actual moat here.

apps.title: Guest Bucket List Builder
apps.description: Guests add items to the couple's shared life bucket list live on their phones, growing on a display wall before becoming a formatted keepsake PDF.
apps.why: Instead of generic advice, guests are asked to imagine the couple's future — participatory, forward-looking, and the output is something the couple actually uses.
cool_features: Categorized suggestions — "places," "experiences," "skills to learn," "scary things," "nights in" — keeps entries specific
Upvoting lets the room boost the wildest ideas to the top of the list
Guests can attach a personal offer alongside the item ("I'll teach you both to sail — Uncle Pete")
Final list gets typeset into a printed hardcover book delivered to the couple two weeks later
Each item stays checkable in the couple's app for years — they log completions as they go
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: Submission is short and ambient — guests add one or two items between courses. The display wall grows visibly through dinner, giving the room a shared thing to glance at without demanding attention.

non_app_version.description: Cards on each table asking guests to write one bucket-list item; MOH collects them in a jar for the couple.
non_app_version.needed: No — cards in a jar cover the sentiment.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Marginally — upvoting and the live-growing wall add spectacle, but the core idea works fine on paper.

apps.title: Table-Specific Conversation Starter Cards
apps.description: Each table scans a unique QR code that serves conversation prompts tailored to that table's guest mix, shared history with the couple, and context notes.
apps.why: Guests feel like the couple thought of them specifically — the prompts reference their table's actual stories, not generic icebreakers.
cool_features: Each guest gets a 30-second profile before the wedding — where they know the couple from, one thing they'd want a stranger to ask them
Prompts pull from those profiles: "You and Marcus both spent time in Antigua — ask him about the year he lived there"
"Surprise me" button auto-suggests a stranger at your table you'd probably click with
Optional anonymous prompt-to-the-table ("ask table 4 who's the loudest snorer")
Post-wedding, every guest gets a booklet of the people they met, with the profiles shared
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: The app causes IRL conversation. Guests use it once during cocktail hour or when they sit down, then have a reason to talk to the stranger across the table for the rest of the night.

non_app_version.description: Printed prompt cards at each table with a set of icebreakers, plus a "how I know the couple" line on each escort card.
non_app_version.needed: No — printed cards are charming and effective.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — "surprise me" matching and a searchable directory create introductions printed cards can't broker mid-conversation.

apps.title: The Prediction Vault
apps.description: Guests submit sealed predictions about the couple's future grouped by year (1, 5, 10, 25 years out), unlocked on each anniversary when the couple scores them.
apps.why: It creates a recurring anniversary ritual — opening predictions with friends and scoring who was right becomes a party game that spans decades.
cool_features: Weighted questions — some worth more points if you get them right ("how many kids" outscores "what city")
Live histogram on screen during dinner: "73% of you think they'll have kids within 3 years. The bride would like the room to know she disagrees."
5-, 10-, and 25-year tiers — guest picks when their prediction unlocks
Every guest gets a personalized "prediction receipt" texted to them so they remember what they bet on
Anniversary emails auto-deliver the year's predictions with a scoring interface for the couple
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: It's not a live entertainment loop — it's a 3-minute submission during cocktail hour, then it disappears. But it keeps giving back to the couple for decades.

non_app_version.description: Prediction cards at each table, sealed in a jar labelled "Open in 5 years."
non_app_version.needed: No — a jar of predictions is a lovely low-tech classic.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — the live histogram, scheduled anniversary emails, and multi-decade tiers are things a jar cannot do.

apps.title: Guest Memory Map
apps.description: Guests pin where they were in the world on the day of key couple milestones, adding a short note, building a live globe of the couple's people on the reception screen.
apps.why: Seeing pins appear in Tokyo, Berlin, and São Paulo in real time makes the global reach of someone's life genuinely visible — and unexpectedly moving.
cool_features: Live 3D globe on the big screen — pins pulse as new ones drop in real time
Each pin can carry a photo of the guest from that day (grandma's polaroid from 1974, dad's college dorm)
Milestone selector — "the day they met," "the day he proposed," "tonight" — same map, different layers
The finished map exports as a large-format print the couple can frame
Optional "who was near who" reveal: which guests were coincidentally in the same city on the same milestone
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: The pinning takes 30 seconds. The payoff is on the wall — the room watches the globe fill up together and half of it involuntarily says "aww" when a new continent lights up.

non_app_version.description: A large printed world map on an easel with pushpins; guests pin their location and write on a sticky note.
non_app_version.needed: No — a physical pin map is a charming keepsake.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — the live-updating globe, photo-per-pin, and coincidence reveals turn a passive artifact into an active room moment.

apps.title: The Live Roast Board
apps.description: Guests submit anonymous roast jokes and funny memories; the MC approves them before they hit the display wall, where the crowd votes on the funniest one.
apps.why: It gives the funny friend a channel without the chaos of an open mic — structured, moderated, and the whole room laughs together without anyone having to stand up uninvited.
cool_features: Best-man moderator dashboard — approve, reject, or edit for length before it hits the wall
Live upvoting — top-voted roast gets read aloud at speeches with credit to the anonymous author
"Tag a target" — roasts can be directed at the bride, groom, or a specific friend at a specific table
Category tiers: "gentle," "medium," "unhinged" — the couple picks which tiers are live
Winning roast gets a printed "Roast Champion" certificate handed out at the end of the night
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: Instead of one drunk uncle at an open mic derailing the night, the whole room contributes and the funniest lines float up naturally. Guests write in 20 seconds, then look up to watch the wall.

non_app_version.description: Open-mic segment during speeches where anyone can grab the mic and tell their best story.
non_app_version.needed: No — open mics happen at weddings all the time.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — moderation prevents disasters, upvoting surfaces the actual funniest bits, and shy funny people finally get a channel.

apps.title: The Couple's Unpopular Opinions Icebreaker
apps.description: The couple pre-loads their real unpopular opinions and guests vote agree/disagree in real time, revealing whether the room sides with the bride or groom on each take.
apps.why: Guests who don't know each other start debating 'pineapple on pizza' before they've finished their welcome drink — instant conversations, no awkwardness.
cool_features: Live "his side / her side" bar that fills in real time as votes come in
Reveal moment: after voting closes, the screen shows which of the couple actually holds the opinion — and the room finds out they're outnumbered
20 opinions total, dropped one at a time across cocktail hour
Table-level minority tag: "Table 6 is the only table in the room that agreed with the groom on ranch dressing"
The couple gets a printed report of which of their takes the world actually endorses
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: It's a 5-second tap between conversations, and the real value is what happens next — tablemates arguing about mayonnaise while the results fill in behind them.

non_app_version.description: Printed ballot at cocktail hour with 20 opinions and a check-box for agree/disagree, results tallied by hand and announced later.
non_app_version.needed: No — a paper ballot works technically.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — live bars filling in real time, per-table breakdowns, and the "who actually said this" reveal are the entire show. Paper ballots kill the pacing.

apps.title: The First Dance Reveal Ballot
apps.description: Guests vote on which of three first dances the couple will actually perform, with a live countdown clock ticking down before the winner is announced on screen.
apps.why: The whole room is invested in the outcome before the dance even starts — the anticipation does what choreography alone can't.
cool_features: Each option shown as a stylized card — song, mood, brief description ("the slow ballad," "the choreographed disco number," "the surprise TikTok dance")
Voting window is exactly 90 seconds with a countdown clock on the big screen for pure suspense
Live bar chart updates as votes come in — the leader flips twice before the buzzer
Loser dances get teased in a montage after: "here's what you would have seen"
The couple pre-rehearsed all three, so whichever wins, they're ready
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: It takes 90 seconds of phone use and pays back with the entire room roaring when the winning song drops. The dance itself is a room moment — no phones out, everyone watching.

non_app_version.description: DJ holds a mic and asks the room to cheer loudest for their favorite of three options; loudest wins.
non_app_version.needed: No — the cheer method works.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — the countdown, live vote chart, and per-guest ballot give the room a genuine cliffhanger a cheer contest can't.

apps.title: Custom Wedding Bingo
apps.description: Every guest gets a unique bingo card with custom squares the couple wrote — 'best man trips on his speech,' 'DJ plays ABBA' — and taps them as they happen in real time.
apps.why: The squares are hilarious because the couple wrote them knowing exactly who will cry and what the DJ will play — and it makes every guest pay attention to everything, all night.
cool_features: Every card is uniquely shuffled — no two guests have the same bingo
Some squares are ultra-specific inside jokes only certain tables will get ("Aunt Linda mentions her book club")
Bingo triggers a small celebratory sound on the winner's phone and a subtle notification to the MC to call it out
Progressive difficulty — 1-line bingo pays a small prize, full-card blackout wins the grand prize
Post-wedding, the couple sees which squares got tapped most (what actually happened) vs. never (what they predicted wrong)
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: It makes guests hyper-observant — instead of scrolling social feeds, they're watching every corner of the room for the next square to hit. Phone use is a single tap per event.

non_app_version.description: Printed bingo cards handed out at arrival, guests mark with a pen and shout "BINGO" when they win.
non_app_version.needed: No — paper bingo is a decades-old party staple.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — unique shuffled cards per guest, real-time win detection, and the "what actually happened" post-wedding data are things paper can't do.

apps.title: The Guest Advice Oracle
apps.description: Guests respond to structured prompts — best marriage advice, a mistake to avoid, something you wish someone had told you — displayed as a live scrolling oracle wall.
apps.why: The output is genuinely useful (real wisdom from people who know you), and the couple can post a live question mid-reception and watch the room respond in real time.
cool_features: Anonymous mode toggle — some advice is more honest when it's not signed
Long-form allowed (up to 500 characters) because this crowd has things to say
Prompts tagged by "years married" — the app asks 5-year-marrieds different questions than 40-year-marrieds
The couple can drop a live question mid-reception ("first fight we're going to have?") and watch the wall fill in
Two printed hardcovers delivered post-wedding — one signed advice, one anonymous
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: It's ambient — guests fill it out when they naturally reach for their phone (waiting for dinner, in line for the bar). Nobody has to do it. The screen presence is subtle.

non_app_version.description: Prompt cards on every table with an advice book to write in — a well-established wedding tradition.
non_app_version.needed: No — the advice book is a genre standard.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Marginally — anonymous mode and years-married tagging pull more honest wisdom, but a book already does 90% of the job well.

apps.title: The Relationship Origin Story Exhibit
apps.description: A beautifully designed digital exhibit of the couple's story — photos, maps, milestones, voice notes — that guests explore on their phones like a mini-museum during cocktail hour.
apps.why: Guests who don't know the full story get genuinely drawn in — it's the 'how did you two meet?' story told properly, with the couple's own voice and photos.
cool_features: Chronological "scroll through the years" mode with photos, texts, and voice notes at each milestone
Voice notes from the couple narrating specific moments ("this is the night I knew")
Interactive map layer showing every place they've lived, every trip that mattered, the exact restaurant of the first date
"Guest annotations" — anyone who was present at a moment can add their own memory to the timeline
Post-wedding, the exhibit stays live at a private URL the couple can share with future family
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: Cocktail hour is exactly when guests want to know the story — they read for 5 minutes, then have things to actually talk about at dinner. It replaces awkward small-talk with informed conversation.

non_app_version.description: Printed timeline boards on easels around the venue with photos and captions guests can walk past.
non_app_version.needed: No — photo timelines are a classic wedding display.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — voice narration, interactive maps, and live guest annotations bring the story to life in ways a printed board cannot.

apps.title: Design Our Future Home Map
apps.description: Guests pin places on an interactive map — the couple's meaningful spots, neighborhoods they should move to, places they must visit — filling live on the reception display.
apps.why: It becomes a living map the couple actually uses after: part artifact, part conversation piece, part shared life plan.
cool_features: Layered pins: "places we've been together," "places to visit," "where we should retire," each in a different color
Guests can attach a one-sentence reason to their pin ("you must eat at this specific ramen shop")
The couple can save any pin into their real Google Maps as a starred location after the wedding
Live "trending city" callouts on the big screen as pins cluster ("Lisbon has 12 pins — the room clearly has opinions")
Final map printed as a framed poster the couple can hang at home
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: Each guest drops one or two pins between courses — 30 seconds of phone use. The map grows live on the wall and gives tables something to point at and argue about.

non_app_version.description: Framed cork map on an easel with colored pins and small note cards guests write on and tack up.
non_app_version.needed: No — a physical pin map is charming and tactile.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — importable-to-Google-Maps pins, layer filtering, and live cluster callouts turn the map from an artifact into a usable tool.

apps.title: The Collaborative Soundtrack
apps.description: Guests submit a song request paired with the memory it represents, votes shape the queue, and as each song plays its story appears on the display wall for everyone to read.
apps.why: The moment 'Dancing Queen' plays and a guest's lake-house memory appears on screen, everyone at that table loses it — the stories are what the music actually means.
cool_features: Pre-loaded with the couple's "must play" and "do not play" lists — guests see instantly if their song is unavailable
DJ has a curator view that lets them accept/reject requests and reorder them
Each song's "story" is timestamped and saved to a keepsake playlist the couple can replay on any anniversary
Special "first dance dedication" slot — guests can submit a dedication read before the first dance, couple picks one anonymously
The on-screen story appears for the length of the song's intro, then fades so the dancing takes over
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: Guests are motivated to actually dance to their song when it plays, because their sentence is on the screen. The dance floor stays full, not empty because everyone's requesting songs and then not dancing.

non_app_version.description: Request slips at the DJ booth with a "why?" line; DJ reads the reason on the mic before dropping the song.
non_app_version.needed: No — slips + mic covers it.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — the on-screen sentence during the intro, the keepsake playlist, and the do-not-play filter are meaningfully better than paper.

apps.title: The Unprompted Love Letter Machine
apps.description: Guests submit secret messages or memories addressed to the bride, groom, or both; at a chosen moment the couple reads them live on screen for the first time, in front of everyone.
apps.why: The raw authenticity of a couple reading unscripted messages from people they love, in real time, is the moment guests most want to witness.
cool_features: Guest chooses one of three "stationery" styles (bride's handwriting, groom's handwriting, or a shared typewriter font)
One-tap prompts if the guest freezes: "Tell them about the first time you met one of them" / "What did you think when you met the other?"
At end of night, all 100+ letters compiled into a real hardbound book, mailed to the couple two weeks later
The maid of honor's tablet lets her pin 3 letters as "read at the anniversary" — the couple gets those emailed on their 1-year anniversary
Letters appear on the big screen typed out one word at a time in the chosen handwriting, like they're being written live
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: Guests spend 90 seconds on their phone once. The rest of the night they're watching the screen with everyone else — and every time a new letter appears, half the room stops talking to read it. It creates group hush moments, not individual scroll moments.

non_app_version.description: Stack of cream cards and pens on every table, a decorated wooden box to drop them in, MOH reads a handful aloud between courses.
non_app_version.needed: No — cards work.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — the live handwritten-reveal on screen is a genuinely new emotional beat cards can't produce.

apps.title: Wedding Day Emotion Pulse
apps.description: Guests are prompted 5–6 times during the day to log how they're feeling, and the aggregate emotion is visualized live on the reception screen as a real-time pulse.
apps.why: Seeing the emotion spike during the vows and first dance — captured as a graph the couple keeps forever — is unexpectedly moving to look at years later.
cool_features: Prompts are timed to moments — one before the ceremony, one after the vows, one at first dance, one at the last song
One-tap emotion slider (from "content" to "actively weeping") — takes 3 seconds per prompt
Live pulse graph on the wall during dinner shows the day's arc as it happens
Final visualization exports as a framed print — a literal emotional profile of the wedding
Optional per-guest heatmap: the couple can see which moments hit hardest for their parents vs. their college friends
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: The prompts arrive at natural pauses (right after big moments), not during them. Each interaction is 3 seconds. The graph is the artifact — everything else is invisible.

non_app_version.description: Nothing directly comparable — you could hand out emotion cards with sliders to fill in and tally them by hand, but the real-time aggregation is the point.
non_app_version.needed: No paper version really captures this.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — the live graph and per-moment aggregation are the entire idea; without them there's nothing to visualize.

apps.title: Table-to-Table Secret Relay
apps.description: Each table writes a secret message or dare for the next table, who must complete it and photograph the result before passing a new one on — a chain of challenges across the room.
apps.why: By the end of cocktail hour, tables that started as strangers have a shared photo and a story — the dare creates the connection no seating chart could engineer.
cool_features: Pre-loaded dare templates ("convince the next table to do a synchronized toast") for tables who need a starter
Photo proof required — every completed dare uploads a picture to a shared feed
Live "chain progress" on the wall — visualize the relay hopping from table 2 → 5 → 8 → 1 in real time
MC moderator dashboard to approve dares before they're passed on (blocks anything genuinely off-side)
Final chain compiled into a photo booklet the couple keeps — one dare, one photo, per page
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: It literally forces tables to interact with each other. Phones are only used to receive/send the dare and photograph the result — the challenge itself is entirely IRL.

non_app_version.description: Sealed envelopes hand-delivered by servers from one table to the next, with dares written inside and Polaroids for photo proof.
non_app_version.needed: No — servers with envelopes could carry the concept.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — live chain tracking on screen, real-time photo uploads, and MC moderation are much cleaner than the envelope logistics.

apps.title: The Personalized Cocktail Quiz
apps.description: Guests take a short personality quiz and are matched to a personalized cocktail from the wedding's custom menu, where every drink is named after a moment in the couple's story.
apps.why: It's a conversation starter at the bar, a way to share the couple's story through the drinks menu, and gives the bartender a live dashboard of what's trending.
cool_features: 5-question quiz — mood, preferred flavor, risk tolerance, favorite past decade, one weirdly personal question the couple picked
Each cocktail card includes the story behind the name ("The 3 AM Kensington: made with gin because that's what they were drinking the night they first said 'I love you'")
Bartender dashboard shows most-ordered drinks live, so they can prep in bulk
Guests can share their match to the group feed with a personalized caption
Post-wedding, the couple gets a printed cocktail menu keepsake with story cards
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: The whole point is to get people to the bar and talking to strangers about their weird cocktail matches. Phone use is a 90-second quiz, then it's cocktail chatter for the rest of the hour.

non_app_version.description: Printed cocktail menu with story cards for each drink; bartender asks guests a couple of quiz-style questions and recommends a drink.
non_app_version.needed: No — a well-designed menu covers a lot of it.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — the personality-match reveal, the shareable card, and the bartender's live trending dashboard turn ordering a drink into a small event.

apps.title: The Parallel Universe Game
apps.description: Guests submit what would have happened to the couple if they'd never met; the best entries are read aloud at dinner before the couple reveals their real 'almost didn't meet' story.
apps.why: It asks guests to think deeply about the couple's story, produces genuinely funny and touching entries, and the couple's real story is usually more dramatic than anyone guesses.
cool_features: Structured prompts: "where would the bride be right now?" / "what would the groom's job be?" / "who would they have married instead?"
Guests upvote the funniest and most-touching submissions — top 5 of each get read at speeches
The couple's real "we almost didn't meet" story is teased on screen throughout dinner ("if the flight had been on time...")
Final reveal is a short couple-narrated video played after the guest entries are read
All submissions compiled into a printed "alternate lives" chapbook the couple keeps
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: Submissions are asynchronous over dinner — guests write between courses. The screen event is a bounded 10-minute segment before the couple's reveal, then it's done.

non_app_version.description: Prompt cards at each table asking guests to write down their "alternate universe" scenario; MOH reads a few aloud during speeches.
non_app_version.needed: No — cards can carry the concept.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Yes — live upvoting surfaces the actually-good entries, on-screen teasers build to the reveal, and the video payoff needs the app to land.

apps.title: Who Said It?
apps.tagline: Guess the sender. Loser buys the next round.
apps.description: A live guessing game: real texts and photos from your relationship, blurred sender, guests vote his side vs her side vs mutual friends.
apps.why: Guests learn the couple through the couple's own voice — chronologically — and the family-vs-family scoreboard turns 'who knows them best' into a room-wide sport with actual stakes.
cool_features: 20 texts across the relationship, chronologically ordered so guests watch the couple's voice change over time
Bonus rounds: "guess the year" for extra points
Optional "spicy round" that requires a special code shared at the friends' tables
Running "who knows them better" scoreboard split between his family, her family, and mutual friends
Reveal card shows the full context around each text (what the couple were arguing about, where they were)
apps.why_it_respects_the_irl_wedding: Everyone's looking at the same screen and laughing at the same time. Phone use is one tap per round, then look up. It's 10 minutes of collective "how did they end up together, honestly."

non_app_version.description: Print the texts on giant cards, MOH reveals one at a time, tables shout "his!" or "hers!" and self-score.
non_app_version.needed: No — a card-based reveal is close to as fun.
non_app_version.adds_to_the_experience: Marginally — real-time voting and family-vs-family scoring are neat, but the core comedy is in the texts themselves, not the tech.
